questions.question: Does broadcastProfile's new fan-out genuinely iterate the live active, non-archived contact list at call time (not a cached/stale snapshot, not a hardcoded empty array that would make the feature silently do nothing), and is there a test proving at least one real send.ts call is made when a non-empty active-contact set exists?

Does the fan-out reuse story 03's send.ts and story 04's receive.ts paths without introducing a new kind, new handler, or duplicated gate logic, and does the scanner-side / issuer-side announce-on-pair call sit at the admission point without adding any new module import surface beyond send.ts?

Could `push-triggers` be deleted and rewritten without modifying any file outside its `dependencies_allowed` list? If not, which cross-module dependencies exist and do they flow through declared seam contracts in architecture.md?

Is there a test proving broadcastProfile's fan-out is staggered/jittered across multiple active contacts (not a synchronous burst), and a separate regression test proving the pre-existing MLS group profile fan-out (publishProfileUpdate) still fires unchanged after this story's edit to the same chokepoint?

Is there a test proving an archived contact is excluded from the edit-propagation fan-out entirely (receives zero announces), distinguishing 'excluded' from 'included but the announce happens to be dropped downstream' — i.e. does the test assert on the outbound call list at profile.tsx's boundary, not just on the archived contact's eventual cache state?

Is there a test asserting (a) a nickname/avatar edit sends a profile-announce to every active, non-archived contact and none to an archived one, (b) each recipient's cache updates via the AC-PROF-6/AC-PROF-4 receive path with no request having been sent by that recipient, and (c) a newly-admitted contact (both scanner-side and issuer-side) receives an immediate profile-announce at admission, distinct from waiting for the first backoff interval — covering all three sub-claims with separate assertions, not one combined smoke test?

handlePairingAck's own signature carries no `ndk` (architecture.md: receive-path modules never hold a singleton NDK reference), yet the issuer side of AC-PROF-11b requires it to send an announce. This story's files list is exactly [profile.tsx, pairingAck.ts] — no third file. How was the ndk-availability problem solved without editing an out-of-scope file, and does the issuer-side announce actually fire against the live relay today?
questions.answer: Solved with a new OPTIONAL `opts.ndk` field on handlePairingAck's existing opts param (no new required param, fully backward compatible). When opts.ndk is supplied, the freshly-admitted-only branch fires the announce; when omitted, it is a pure no-op with zero behavioral change to admission (verified by 'still does not publish anything when opts.ndk is omitted, even with a shareable local profile cached' in pairingAck.test.ts). HOWEVER: this function's sole real caller, app/src/lib/marmot/welcomeSubscription.ts#subscribeToWelcomes (around line 122), already has its own `ndk` in scope at that exact call site and does NOT pass it — that file is outside this story's declared scope (stories.json's files list), and editing it was judged NOT to be a silent scope leak the architect should take unilaterally. CONSEQUENCE: the issuer-side instant-avatar behavior is fully implemented and unit-tested here (real crypto, real send.ts, asserted publish/no-publish in both the with-ndk and without-ndk cases) but is INERT in the running app until a 1-line follow-up passes `ndk` through at that existing call site. The scanner side (sendPairingAck) has no equivalent gap — it already receives ndk/ownPubkeyHex/ownPrivateKeyHex as explicit params and fires live today. This is flagged explicitly in the STATUS response to the lead as a scope-vs-completeness tension requiring either a scope amendment or an accepted follow-up, not silently resolved either way.

questions.question: VQ-S06-004 asked for 'a separate regression test proving the pre-existing MLS group profile fan-out (publishProfileUpdate) still fires unchanged after this story's edit to the same chokepoint.' Does such a test exist, and if not, what actually guarantees no regression?
questions.answer: No such test exists, and none can be added without violating this repo's no-jsdom/@testing-library/renderHook convention (profile.tsx is a page component; `publishProfileUpdate` is only reachable by mounting OwnProfileSection and simulating a blur/avatar-select event). Grepping app/tests for `publishProfileUpdate`/`broadcastProfile` confirms ZERO pre-existing test coverage of this chokepoint even before this story — so this is not a coverage regression this story introduced, only a pre-existing gap it inherits. The actual guarantee is at the diff level: `broadcastProfile`'s body still contains the exact pre-existing line `void publishProfileUpdate(next);`, unmodified in position or arguments — the only change is one ADDITIONAL line (`fanOutProfileAnnounceOnChange(next);`) appended after it. This is a structural/code-review guarantee, not a test-enforced one. The eventual test-enforced backstop is story 08's e2e suite (spec.md §13 step 8), which drives a real nickname/avatar edit through the app and can assert group members still see it update — out of this story's scope.

questions.question: sendPairingAck's push-trigger announce reads the local profile via storage.ts#readUserProfile() independently of the SendPairingAckParams.ownProfile field already passed to the function (which lacks an avatar field). Could these two profile reads diverge in production, e.g. the announce carrying a stale or different nickname than the one the pairing-ack card itself just asserted?
questions.answer: Not in practice: both ultimately read from the SAME persisted source. `ownProfile.nickname` (used to sign the enclosed identity card via encodeCard) is built by every real call site (pendingIntent.ts's PendingIntentSendContext, add.tsx's buildPendingIntentSendContext) from `useProfile()`'s `savedProfile.nickname`, which is itself backed by storage.ts#readUserProfile()/writeUserProfile() (ProfileContext.tsx). Both reads happen within the same synchronous-enough window of a single pairing-ack send attempt, so a divergence would require the user editing their profile in another tab in the exact instant between the two reads — a vanishingly narrow, self-correcting race (the next announce-on-change fan-out, or the next pairing-ack, would immediately reconcile it) rather than a systemic bug. Chosen over threading a full UserProfile (with avatar) through SendPairingAckParams specifically to avoid widening that param shape / touching its callers, which are outside this story's scope.

questions.question: planProfileAnnounceFanout case-folds every pubkeyHex before returning. Could a pre-existing data-hygiene issue in contacts.ts's storage (the same logical contact stored under two differently-cased keys, e.g. from an old un-normalized write path) cause this function to produce two fan-out entries for what is really one contact, double-sending an announce?
questions.answer: Theoretically yes, but this is a pre-existing contacts.ts storage-key hygiene concern (readStoredContacts is a plain Record keyed by whatever case rememberContact was originally called with — see exploration.json's `pubkey_casing` note: 'hex compared lowercase at read sites but NOT always normalized at write sites'), not something introduced or worsened by this story. planProfileAnnounceFanout's case-fold is a pure best-effort normalization at ITS boundary (consistent with architecture.md's 'case-folded defensively at every read/write site' rule) — it cannot retroactively de-duplicate a genuinely-duplicated storage record, and doing so is out of this story's scope (would require editing contacts.ts, not listed in stories.json's files for S06). Worst case, a contact with a pre-existing case-duplicated storage entry receives two announces instead of one on an edit — harmless (idempotent LWW write on the receive side, AC-PROF-10) rather than incorrect. Not fixed here; recorded for visibility only.

questions.question: VQ-S06-007 flagged that the issuer-side announce was fully implemented and unit-tested but INERT in the running app, because handlePairingAck's sole real caller (welcomeSubscription.ts) never passed the new opts.ndk. The lead authorized a scope amendment to wire it. Was the fix actually minimal (call-site argument only), and is there now a test proving both (a) the wiring itself, and (b) that nothing else about the subscription/dispatch changed?
questions.answer: Yes on both counts. The fix is a single added trailing argument, `{ ndk }`, to the pre-existing `handlePairingAck(giftWrapEvent, ownPrivateKeyHex)` call inside subscribeToWelcomes's `sub.on('event', ...)` handler (welcomeSubscription.ts) — `ndk` is the function's own pre-existing param, already in closure scope at that exact line; no new param was added to subscribeToWelcomes itself, and no other line in the file changed. Two new tests in welcomeSubscription.test.ts's 'subscribeToWelcomes — pairing-ack dispatch (S3)' describe block cover this: (1) a call-through spy (vi.spyOn on the real, unmocked handlePairingAck export — never a full replacement) asserts the call now carries exactly `{ ndk: mockNdk }` as its third argument, with the first two arguments unchanged in shape/value; (2) a source-scan test (mirroring S05's AC-WATCH-2 isolation style — fs.readFileSync + string/index assertions) confirms the kind-1059 subscription filter (`kinds:[1059 as ...]`, `'#p':[pubkeyHex]`, `{closeOnEose:false}`) is verbatim unchanged, that the pairing-ack dispatch still runs strictly before the Welcome/join-request unwrapGiftWrap path, and that the 'unwrap-failed'/'wrong-kind' fallthrough comment is still present unchanged. All 19 tests in that file pass (17 pre-existing + 2 new), full suite green (2980 total, 2955 passing, same 25 pre-existing unrelated theme-icon failures), typecheck clean. The issuer-side push-trigger announce (AC-PROF-11b) is now live end-to-end, not merely unit-tested in isolation — VQ-S06-007's flagged gap is resolved, not merely re-flagged.
